title: Feasibility study of continuous electronic Pomeranchuk cooling with a flavor-degenerate Wigner crystal
authors: Robin J. Dolleman, Ammon Fischer, Lennart Klebl, Alexander Rothstein, Dante M. Kennes, Bernd Beschoten, Florian Libisch, Christoph Stampfer
abstract: Achieving sub-millikelvin electron temperatures in nanoelectronic devices could unveil new transport phenomena, extend quantum coherence times, and enhance the precision of quantum metrology. However, maintaining such low temperatures continuously remains a long-standing challenge. Here, we propose and simulate an on-chip cooling cycle that harnesses the entropy difference between an electron liquid (EL) and a Wigner crystal (WC) in flavor-degenerate flat-band materials. Cooling is driven by a current through a device with a locally gated region. Within this region, the charge carrier density is tuned such that a WC forms beneath the gate. As carriers transition from an EL to WC phase, their entropy increases, extracting heat and the sliding WC advects this heat along the device. The heat is then released when carriers transition back to the EL phase, which establishes distinct hot and cold regions and a steady temperature gradient over the device. Simulations show net cooling for sufficiently low current densities, typically below $1~\mathrm{nA}/μ\mathrm{m}$, whereas Joule heating dominates at higher currents. Within the gated region, we estimate cooling powers of up to $8.4~\mathrm{aW}/μ\mathrm{m}$ at a bath temperature of $4~\mathrm{mK}$. Our approach can achieve electron temperatures well below $1~\mathrm{mK}$ under suitable conditions, promising a route towards continuous on-chip cooling in this temperature regime. Our approach applies to any flat-band material with low-energy flavor degeneracy (valley and/or orbital) and low disorder, including gapped Bernal-stacked bilayer graphene, rhombohedral-stacked multilayer graphene, and magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene.
summary: This paper proposes a novel method for continuous, on-chip electronic cooling by exploiting the large entropy difference between an Electron Liquid and a Wigner Crystal phase in 2D materials. By tuning the carrier density to induce this phase transition, heat is extracted from the electron system, potentially reaching temperatures below $1 	ext{ mK}$. The analysis uses advanced thermal transport simulations to model this non-equilibrium cooling cycle.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving continuous, on-chip cooling of electron temperatures to sub-millikelvin levels in nanoelectronic devices.
main_result: The authors propose a cooling cycle based on the entropy difference between an Electron Liquid (EL) and a Wigner Crystal (WC) phase, demonstrating potential cooling powers up to $8.4 	ext{ aW}/\mu	ext{m}$ below $1 	ext{ mK}$.
method: The cooling is modeled by simulating a 1D convection-diffusion equation that accounts for heat sources from Joule heating, entropic cooling ($\Delta S$), and phonon coupling.
model_or_system: The system is a 2D, flavor-degenerate material (like multilayer graphene) where the charge carriers undergo a phase transition between an EL and a WC when the local gate voltage is tuned.
key_observables: Cooling power ($\dot{Q}_{	ext{cool}}$), minimum attainable temperature ($T_{	ext{min}}$), and the temperature profile $T(x)$.
important_parameters: Low current densities ($< 1 	ext{ nA}/\mu	ext{m}$), WC density ($n_{WC}$), and the bath temperature ($T_{	ext{bath}}$).
main_assumptions: The cooling mechanism relies on the entropy difference ($\Delta S = S_{WC} - S_{EL}$), and the model assumes the validity of the Wiedemann-Franz relation in the EL phase while accounting for phonon backflow in the WC phase.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the WC lattice structure, band structures for graphene variants, the entropy difference between EL and WC, and simulation results showing the temperature profile and minimum attainable temperature.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical problem, details the EL-WC phase transition mechanism driven by entropy, derives the thermodynamic cycle using the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, and finally simulates the heat transport using a 1D convection-diffusion equation.
why_interesting: This work connects fundamental concepts of phase transitions (WC formation) and entropy maximization/minimization to a practical, non-equilibrium thermodynamic process (cooling), which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and energy dissipation studies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Illustration of the triangular lattice of charge carriers in the WC. Each charge carrier in confined within a potential created by the surrounding carriers. (b) Exemplary band structures of magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBG) and gapped rhombohedrally stacked multilayer graphene (rMLG); for two layers, rMLG corresponds to Bernal-stacked bilayer graphene. Depending on the system, each carrier can carry an orbital and/or valley degree of freedom close to the band gaps. (c) Evolution of the four lowest energy states of a confined charge carrier in a valley-degenerate system as a function of out-of-plane magnetic field (schematically). K and K′ indicate the valleys and...

FIG. 2. (a) Sketch of the proposed thermodynamic cycle as an ideal isothermal process in the entropy–temperature diagram. (b) Schematic of the locally top-gated multilayer graphene device and the simplified one-dimensional model used for the simulations. (c) Exemplary spatial distribution of nEL and nWC that is assumed for the simulations.

FIG. 3. Simulations to determine the minimum possible electron temperature. Fixed simulation parameters are the electrical resistivity Rs = 1 kΩ, bath temperature Tbath = 4 mK. (a) Temperature in the cold region as a function of bias current for different values of the relative Lorenz number λWC. (b) Exemplary temperature T(x) for different values of the bias current I, for the case nEL = 2.6 × 1011 cm−2, nWC = 4.88 × 1010 cm−2 and λWC = 1 × 10−6. (c) Temperature in the cold region as a function of bias current for different densities nWC in units of cm−2, assuming λWC = 1 × 10−6. (d) Minimal attainable temperature as a function of nWC.

FIG. 4. Cooling power and its dependence on sheet resistance, Tbath = 4 mK and λWC = 1 × 10−6. (a) Cooling power ˙Qcool versus current for several sheet resistances Rs, the colours correspond to panel (b). Inset shows the temperature profile T(x) for currents I = 25 pA and I = 100 pA. (b) The maximum cooling power ˙Qmax as a function of Rs. (c) ˙Qcool versus current for different densities nWC (units cm−2). With increasing nWC the cooling power initially increases (left panel), reaches a maximum and then rapidly decreases (right panel). The colour scale corresponds to panel (d). (d) Maximum cooling power ˙Qmax as a function of nWC.

FIG. 5. (a) Relative Lorenz number λWC versus temperature for a sample lengths L = 1 µm and L = 10 µm. (b) Contribution of the phonon branches to the entropy per particle. Inset shows the two phonon branches.
